references.title: Revealing online learning Behaviors and activity patterns and making predictions with data mining techniques in online teaching
references.journal: Journal of Online Learning and Teaching

extracted.Features: [UserID, LoginFre, LastLog, ClassFre, LastCall, NoPosting, DisFre, ReadHr, ReadMegs]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Dataset was cleaned with removal of all rows/columns with issues.

Creation of a session filter and feature extraction. Calculations were done on daily and weekly basis.
extracted.Comments:
Does 2 tasks: Description - both from mere statistics to ARM and Clustering. The second task is Prediction of online learning performance - the grade.

Authors used bth Weka and Knime. 

Performance of the predictive model is not tested on a separate set nor is the model’s ability to get predictions right assessed. 

Instead, authors merely focus on the interpretability of the branches of the DT regardless of the performance.
extracted.Limitations:
1. No discernible model assessment strategy.

2. Lack of tools for analysis to help instructors identify individual or group learning patterns. 

3. Few and features  are used.

extracted.Comments:
3 groups - Clusters 1 and 2 have better performance than cluster 3. 

Cluster 3 was later subdivided into 2 distinct groups.

Rule 1 had more than 50% support and 90% confidence. 
All subsequent rules are clearly below

annotations.text: Work uses multiple data mining techniques on data from 98 undergraduate students. Authors distinguish between active and passive learners and then extract features for predictive modeling.

Instructors rely on LMS to provide basic data in the interactions between student and the contents. This makes this sort of data especially appealing. However, at the time of the paper, there is limited research going into the data generated by LMS. However, the potential of the statistical analysis of these logs is recognized.
Undergraduate Business Software Applications course as part of a 4 year vocational track. The course follows a PBL approach. In total the interactions of 98 students across 6 weeks was recorded.
Authors extract multiple features and then pass the data over 2 distinct types of analysis:

Distribution of login frequency and frequency of access to course materials resembled a normal distribution. Moreover, there was positive skew in some of the other variables. Most important variables were found to be - NP, NR and FAC.
For daily learning patterns, the authors used ARM. For students who made daily logins rule 1 was that more than 50% of activities were reading course materials and announcements.

The decision tree shows that frequency of accessing course materials was the most important variable. This was a regression approach using a Decision Tree. The authors however never test the performance of the model on either this set or a separate set at all. Instead, they merely focus on interpreting the factors that contribute to one outcomes vs the other.
